title: Willow plant pot sleeve
subtitle: A woven willow sleeve to dress a terracotta pot
excerpt: A willow sleeve that wraps a standard terracotta pot: worked on the pot itself as a mould, with vertical stakes and a simple randing weave. No base is needed; the pot base shows. The sleeve transforms a plain pot into a decorative indoor or outdoor container. A first randing project.
sourceNotes: Victorian domestic craft manuals for pot covers and sleeve work; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) references woven covers for household pots.

content.text: Working a willow sleeve on the pot itself solves the most common beginner problem in basketry: how to keep the weaving upright and evenly spaced without a permanent frame. The pot is the mould. When the sleeve is finished and dry, it lifts off cleanly and can be transferred to another pot of the same size.

Brown or buff willow, 2-foot lengths for a standard 15 cm (6 inch) pot, 3-foot lengths for a 20 cm (8 inch) pot. Soak the willow in cold water for 1 hour before use. A standard 15 cm pot needs 16 stakes and approximately 120 g of weaving material.
: insert 16 stakes (2-foot lengths, butt end down) into the soil of the pot, evenly spaced around the circumference, close to the pot wall. Press them 4 to 5 cm into the soil so they stand upright.
at the base, close to the pot surface. This upsett locks the stakes into position. Ensure the weaving sits against the pot side rather than pulling the stakes inward.
Continue randing upward to the pot rim. Join new rods by inserting the new tip behind the stake where the old tip ended, overlapping by 2 to 3 stakes before continuing.

At the rim, work 2 rows of 3-rod waling to stiffen the top edge. Then turn the stakes down into the weaving to form the border: take each stake in turn, bend it over the next stake and tuck the tip behind the second stake. Trim the ends inside the sleeve.
Leave the sleeve on the pot to dry for 24 hours. Once dry, the sleeve holds its shape and lifts off cleanly.
Taller sleeve: use 3-foot stakes and weave to 10 cm above the pot rim; the top section flares outward as a decorative collar.
Dyed willow accent: one in four stakes in smoked or boiled willow of a contrasting colour creates a regular vertical stripe in the randing.

Outdoor sleeves exposed to weather will last 3 to 5 years before the border and base rows deteriorate. Indoor sleeves last indefinitely. Re-wet and reshape if the sleeve becomes misshapen from a knock.

items.fix: Ease the weaving tension; each row should sit snugly against the stake without pulling it toward the centre. If stakes are already pulled in, use the bodkin to lever them back to the pot wall before continuing.
items.cause: Weaving rod pulled too tightly around the stake
items.symptom: Stakes pull inward as randing proceeds

items.fix: Soak the top 5 cm of the pot soil with water to soften it before attempting to lift the sleeve. For future sleeves, press stakes only 3 cm into the soil.
items.cause: Stakes driven too deeply into the soil; or soil dried and shrank around the stake ends
items.symptom: Sleeve will not lift off the pot after drying
